annotations.label: Euclidean distance

anchor.snippet: the Euclidean distance between two points in a Euclidean space is the length of the line segment between them

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.dist_eq gives dist x y = √(∑ i, dist (x i) (y i) ^ 2) for the standard Euclidean space.

annotations.label: Distance between non-point objects

anchor.snippet: The distance between two objects that are not points is usually defined to be the smallest distance among pairs of points from the two objects

annotations.note: Metric.infDist defines the infimum of distances from a point to a set; the symmetric set-to-set version is via Metric.hausdorffDist-related infrastructure rather than a named decl.

annotations.label: Distance on the real line

anchor.snippet: The distance between any two points on the real line is the absolute value of the numerical difference of their coordinates

annotations.note: Real.dist_eq states dist x y = |x - y| on ℝ.

annotations.label: Alternative one-dimensional formula

anchor.snippet: A more complicated formula, giving the same value, but generalizing more readily to higher dimensions

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.dist_eq specialized to Fin 1 yields the √((x-y)²) formula, but no dedicated lemma is stated.

annotations.label: Distance in the Euclidean plane

anchor.snippet: In the Euclidean plane

annotations.note: Specializing EuclideanSpace.dist_eq to Fin 2 yields the planar Euclidean distance √((x₁-y₁)² + (x₂-y₂)²).

annotations.label: Derivation via Pythagorean theorem

anchor.snippet: This can be seen by applying the Pythagorean theorem to a right triangle with horizontal and vertical sides

annotations.note: The Pythagorean theorem in Euclidean geometry is formalized but no specific derivation of the planar distance formula from it is stated as a lemma.

annotations.label: Formula via Pythagorean addition

anchor.snippet: In terms of the Pythagorean addition operation

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define a named `Pythagorean addition` operation a ⊕ b = √(a² + b²).

annotations.label: Distance from polar coordinates

anchor.snippet: It is also possible to compute the distance for points given by polar coordinates

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma expresses Euclidean distance between two points in polar form via the law of cosines.

annotations.label: Distance for complex numbers

anchor.snippet: expressed as complex numbers in the complex plane

annotations.note: Complex.dist_eq gives dist x y = ‖x - y‖ (= |x - y|) for x, y : ℂ.

annotations.label: Distance in three dimensions

anchor.snippet: In three dimensions, for points given by their Cartesian coordinates, the distance is

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.dist_eq specialized to Fin 3 yields the standard 3D distance formula.

annotations.label: Distance in n-dimensional Euclidean space

anchor.snippet: In general, for points given by Cartesian coordinates in

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.dist_eq directly states dist x y = √(∑ i, dist (x i) (y i) ^ 2).

annotations.label: Distance via Euclidean norm

anchor.snippet: The Euclidean distance may also be expressed more compactly in terms of the Euclidean norm of the Euclidean vector difference

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.norm_eq combined with the general dist_eq_norm gives dist x y = ‖x - y‖.

annotations.label: Distance between non-point objects (formula list)

anchor.snippet: For pairs of objects that are not both points, the distance can most simply be defined as the smallest distance between any two points from the two objects

annotations.note: Metric.infDist captures point-to-set infimum distance; explicit closed-form distances for lines, planes, spheres, etc. are not given as a single list.

annotations.label: Parallel curve via point-to-curve distance

anchor.snippet: The distance from a point to a curve can be used to define its parallel curve

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of parallel curve.

annotations.label: Symmetry of Euclidean distance

anchor.snippet: It is symmetric

annotations.note: dist_comm holds in any pseudometric space, including EuclideanSpace.

annotations.label: Positivity of Euclidean distance

anchor.snippet: It is positive , meaning that the distance between every two distinct points is a positive number

annotations.note: dist_pos states 0 < dist x y ↔ x ≠ y in any MetricSpace.

annotations.label: Triangle inequality

anchor.snippet: It obeys the triangle inequality

annotations.note: dist_triangle x y z : dist x z ≤ dist x y + dist y z holds in every pseudometric space.

annotations.label: Ptolemy's inequality

anchor.snippet: Another property, Ptolemy's inequality , concerns the Euclidean distances among four points

annotations.note: EuclideanGeometry.mul_dist_le_mul_dist_add_mul_dist proves |AC|·|BD| ≤ |AB|·|CD| + |BC|·|AD|.

annotations.label: Beckman–Quarles theorem

anchor.snippet: According to the Beckman–Quarles theorem

annotations.note: No mention of Beckman–Quarles theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Squared Euclidean distance

anchor.snippet: The value resulting from this omission is the square of the Euclidean distance, and is called the squared Euclidean distance

annotations.note: Squaring EuclideanSpace.dist_eq yields ∑ i, dist (x i) (y i) ^ 2, but no dedicated `sq_dist` lemma is named.

annotations.label: Euclidean minimum spanning tree

anchor.snippet: the Euclidean minimum spanning tree can be determined using only the ordering between distances

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Euclidean minimum spanning tree construction.

annotations.label: Method of least squares

anchor.snippet: it is used in the method of least squares

annotations.note: No least-squares decl found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Squared distance is not a metric

anchor.snippet: Squared Euclidean distance does not form a metric space, as it does not satisfy the triangle inequality

annotations.note: No statement that squared distance fails the triangle inequality is recorded in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convexity of squared distance

anchor.snippet: However it is a smooth, strictly convex function of the two points

annotations.note: Strict convexity of x ↦ x^n for even n is available, but no lemma directly establishes strict convexity of (x,y) ↦ ‖x-y‖².

annotations.label: Euclidean distance matrix

anchor.snippet: The collection of all squared distances between pairs of points from a finite set may be stored in a Euclidean distance matrix

annotations.note: No Euclidean distance matrix construction in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclidean norm

anchor.snippet: its distance is associated with a norm called the Euclidean norm , defined as the distance of each vector from the origin

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace.norm_eq gives ‖x‖ = √(∑ i, ‖x i‖ ^ 2).

annotations.label: Dvoretzky's theorem

anchor.snippet: By Dvoretzky's theorem , every finite-dimensional normed vector space has a high-dimensional subspace on which the norm is approximately Euclidean

annotations.note: No Dvoretzky theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: L2 norm extension

anchor.snippet: It can be extended to infinite-dimensional vector spaces as the L 2 norm or L 2 distance

annotations.note: The `lp` space (and MeasureTheory.Lp) provides the L² norm/distance on infinite-dimensional sequence/function spaces.

annotations.label: Euclidean topology

anchor.snippet: The Euclidean distance gives Euclidean space the structure of a topological space , the Euclidean topology

annotations.note: EuclideanSpace is a MetricSpace and thus carries the induced (Euclidean) topology automatically.

annotations.label: Chebyshev distance

anchor.snippet: Chebyshev distance

annotations.note: PiLp ∞ encodes the sup (Chebyshev) distance via the standard Pi metric structure; no decl is named `Chebyshev`.

annotations.label: Taxicab distance

anchor.snippet: Taxicab distance

annotations.note: PiLp 1 yields the taxicab (ℓ¹) distance, but no `Taxicab` decl is named in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Minkowski distance

anchor.snippet: Minkowski distance

annotations.note: PiLp p (n → 𝕜) realizes the Minkowski ℓᵖ distance for any 1 ≤ p ≤ ∞.

annotations.label: Geodesic distance

anchor.snippet: the Euclidean distance should be distinguished from the geodesic distance, the length of a shortest curve that belongs to the surface

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general intrinsic/geodesic distance on submanifolds or surfaces.
